tasks.title: Cache queue JSON-repair regexes to reduce hot-path CPU in parallel selection
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: performance and cost.

Why it matters:
- Queue selection in parallel mode repeatedly triggers JSON repair/validation paths; recompiling regex patterns each invocation burns CPU and increases lock-hold time.

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- Hoist repair regexes in queue repair logic to static lazy-initialized values and keep behavior identical.

Expansion:
- Add a lightweight benchmark harness for queue-load hot paths to prevent regressions.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: subtle behavior changes in replacement order.
- Mitigation: retain existing repair tests and add targeted ordering regression tests.
evidence: path: crates/ralph/src/queue.rs :: attempt_json_repair :: compiles multiple Regex::new patterns inside the function on each call.
path: crates/ralph/src/commands/run/parallel/worker.rs :: select_next_task_locked :: calls load_queue_with_repair_and_validate for each selection pass.
workflow: ralph run loop --parallel 4 :: repeated selection attempts amplify queue-repair CPU overhead in worker hot paths.
external: https://docs.rs/regex/latest/regex/ :: accessed 2026-02-12 :: regex docs recommend compiling once (e.g., lazy static) instead of recompiling in loops for performance.
plan: Move repair regex definitions to static lazy values and preserve existing replacement order semantics.
Refactor attempt_json_repair to reuse compiled patterns without changing external behavior.
Add regression tests for complex multi-error repair inputs to confirm equivalent outputs.
Optionally add a micro-benchmark or timing assertion in non-CI tooling for queue load path visibility.
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph queue::tests` and `cargo test -p ralph commands::run::parallel::worker` and confirming identical repair behavior with reduced repeated regex compilation.

tasks.title: Rework `ralph task --help` into workflow-oriented command groups
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: UX improvement.

Why it matters:
- `ralph task` is a primary entry point, but current help output presents a long flat command list that increases onboarding friction and misfires on adjacent commands (`field` vs `edit`, `status` vs `done`).

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- Reorganize task subcommands into explicit groups (create/build, lifecycle, edit, relationships, batch/template) with concise one-line intent labels.

Expansion:
- Add short "common journeys" section (create task, start task, complete task, split task) and keep docs aligned.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: help contract tests may fail due to wording drift.
- Mitigation: update contract tests and docs in the same change.
evidence: workflow: cargo run -q -p ralph -- task --help :: renders a long flat command list (20+ subcommands) with no workflow grouping.
path: crates/ralph/src/cli/task/args.rs :: TaskCommand enum :: mixes creation, lifecycle, field edits, relations, and batching in one linear command surface.
workflow: cargo run -q -p ralph -- task --help :: nearby commands with similar names increase cognitive load for first-time users.
plan: Define explicit task command groups and assign each subcommand to one group with concise descriptions.
Update clap help text and after_long_help examples to reflect common user journeys.
Add or update help-output contract tests to lock in grouped layout and key examples.
Refresh CLI docs to mirror the grouped command model.
Verify by running `cargo run -p ralph -- task --help` and confirming grouped sections plus updated examples, then run `cargo test -p ralph` for help contract coverage.

tasks.title: Add `ralph daemon logs` for integrated tail/follow and recent-error views
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: UX improvement and reliability.

Why it matters:
- Daemon users currently have to leave Ralph and manually tail log files, which slows recovery when queue execution stalls or webhook notifications fail.

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- Add `ralph daemon logs` subcommand with `--tail`, `--follow`, and `--since` options against `.ralph/logs/daemon.log`.

Expansion:
- Add structured filtering (`--level`, `--contains`) and a `--json` mode for automation.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: platform/path differences for log locations.
- Mitigation: resolve paths from existing config/runtime conventions and add cross-platform tests.
evidence: path: crates/ralph/src/cli/daemon.rs :: DaemonCommand :: exposes only Start/Stop/Status (plus hidden Serve), no log inspection command.
workflow: cargo run -q -p ralph -- daemon --help :: no built-in way to stream daemon logs.
workflow: docs/guides/advanced.md :: recommends manual `tail -f .ralph/logs/daemon.log` for log access.
path: docs/workflow.md :: Daemon Mode :: documents daemon.log output but no CLI affordance to inspect it.
plan: Add a `logs` daemon subcommand with options for tail count, follow mode, and time window filtering.
Implement robust log-path resolution and file-not-found guidance for uninitialized workspaces.
Add CLI tests for help output and command behavior in empty/log-populated scenarios.
Update docs/examples to prefer `ralph daemon logs` over manual shell tail where appropriate.
Verify by running `cargo run -p ralph -- daemon logs --help` and `cargo test -p ralph daemon`, then manually confirm follow mode against `.ralph/logs/daemon.log`.

tasks.title: Add bulk GitHub issue sync for queue publishing workflows
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: integration and workflow coverage.

Why it matters:
- Publishing tasks to GitHub currently requires one command per task, creating high toil for backlog seeding and recurring synchronization.

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- Extend `ralph queue issue` with bulk mode (`publish-many` or flags on `publish`) supporting filters (`--status`, `--tag`, `--id-pattern`) and dry-run preview.

Expansion:
- Add incremental sync mode that updates only changed tasks and reports summary counts.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: accidental mass issue edits.
- Mitigation: default dry-run summaries, explicit confirmation in interactive mode, and idempotency checks via stored issue URL/number.
evidence: path: crates/ralph/src/cli/queue/issue.rs :: module docs :: explicitly states bulk publish is not handled (single task only).
path: crates/ralph/src/cli/queue/issue.rs :: QueueIssueCommand :: only `Publish` subcommand exists.
workflow: ralph queue issue publish <TASK_ID> :: backlog sync requires repeated manual invocation per task.
plan: Design bulk publish/sync command shape with filter inputs and safe defaults (dry-run first).
Implement batch iteration under queue lock with robust per-task error handling and summary output.
Persist GitHub metadata idempotently in task custom_fields and skip unchanged tasks when possible.
Add integration tests using dry-run and mocked gh interactions for create/update paths.
Verify by running `cargo run -p ralph -- queue issue publish --help` and bulk dry-run commands, then `cargo test -p ralph queue::issue`.

tasks.title: Add webhook delivery diagnostics and replay controls
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: reliability and integration.

Why it matters:
- Ralph supports asynchronous webhooks with retry/backpressure, but operators lack first-class tooling to inspect queue depth, failure counts, and replay failed events. This blocks confident production integrations.

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- Add `ralph webhook status` with delivery queue metrics and recent failures, plus `ralph webhook replay` for controlled retries from persisted failure records.

Expansion:
- Add JSON export for observability pipelines and per-event latency histograms.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: replaying malformed payloads repeatedly.
- Mitigation: bounded replay attempts, explicit event/id targeting, and safe dry-run preview.
evidence: path: crates/ralph/src/cli/webhook.rs :: module responsibilities :: only `webhook test` is implemented; persistent webhook state is not handled.
workflow: cargo run -q -p ralph -- webhook --help :: command surface has only `test` subcommand.
config: docs/configuration.md :: agent.webhook includes retry_count/retry_backoff_ms/queue_capacity/queue_policy but no built-in CLI diagnostics workflow.
plan: Define webhook diagnostics data model (queue depth, dropped count, retry/failure stats, recent failed events).
Add CLI subcommands for status and replay with dry-run and event/task filters.
Persist enough failure metadata for deterministic replay without exposing secrets.
Add tests for status output formats and replay safety limits.
Verify by running `cargo run -p ralph -- webhook status --format json` and `cargo test -p ralph webhook`, confirming inspectable metrics and bounded replay behavior.

tasks.title: Parse queue changes directly in RalphMac watcher path to reduce CLI churn
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: performance and UX improvement.

Why it matters:
- The macOS app already watches `.ralph` files, but each change still triggers CLI subprocesses (`queue list`, `config show`) for reloads. Rapid edits produce avoidable process overhead, slower UI updates, and extra contention.

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- On queue/done file change, parse local JSON first in-app and update task state directly; keep CLI fallback on parse errors or schema mismatch.

Expansion:
- Add incremental diff application for large queues to minimize full-array replacements and UI recompute.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: app parser drift from CLI schema behavior.
- Mitigation: share schema-derived models/tests and fallback to CLI on decode failure.
evidence: path: apps/RalphMac/RalphCore/QueueFileWatcher.swift :: Responsibilities :: watcher already detects queue/done/config file changes but does not parse contents.
path: apps/RalphMac/RalphCore/Workspace.swift :: handleExternalFileChange :: always calls loadTasks and loadRunnerConfiguration on any watched-file change.
path: apps/RalphMac/RalphCore/Workspace.swift :: loadTasks :: invokes CLI `queue list --format json` even for local file-watcher events.
workflow: editing .ralph/queue.json in short bursts :: repeated CLI spawns create lag and unnecessary process overhead in the app.
plan: Implement direct queue/done JSON decode path for file-watcher-triggered refreshes.
Retain CLI fallback path for decode failures and config-resolution-dependent fields.
Add unit tests covering valid decode, malformed JSON fallback, and conflict detection behavior.
Instrument refresh latency before/after to ensure measurable improvement.
Verify by running `make macos-test` and manually editing `.ralph/queue.json`, confirming low-latency UI refresh without repeated CLI invocation on valid changes.

tasks.title: Add aggregated analytics CLI endpoint to cut RalphMac process fan-out
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: performance and cost.

Why it matters:
- The analytics screen launches multiple CLI processes in parallel for summary/velocity/burndown/stats/history, which increases latency and battery usage and multiplies failure points.

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- Add a single aggregated analytics command (for example `ralph queue dashboard --format json --days N`) that returns all currently-needed dashboard payloads in one invocation.

Expansion:
- Add cached snapshots and optional per-section toggles for lightweight polling.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: larger payload may fail whole response when one section errors.
- Mitigation: section-level error envelopes in the aggregated response.
evidence: path: apps/RalphMac/RalphCore/Workspace.swift :: loadAnalytics :: starts five async CLI tasks for dashboard data.
path: apps/RalphMac/RalphCore/Workspace.swift :: loadProductivitySummary/loadVelocity/loadBurndown/loadQueueStats/loadHistory :: each method runs a separate `runAndCollect` process.
workflow: opening analytics dashboard :: multiple process startups increase latency, CPU, and error surface compared with single-response fetch.
plan: Design aggregated dashboard response schema covering current analytics sections with per-section status fields.
Implement a new CLI command that computes and emits the combined JSON payload in one process.
Switch RalphMac analytics loader to the aggregated endpoint with fallback to per-command calls behind feature flag.
Add CLI + app tests validating payload compatibility and graceful partial failures.
Verify by running `cargo run -p ralph -- queue dashboard --format json --days 30` and `make macos-test`, confirming the app reduces subprocess count while preserving analytics content.

tasks.title: Introduce queue snapshot caching for parallel scheduler selection
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: performance and reliability.

Why it matters:
- Parallel scheduling repeatedly reloads and revalidates queue+done files while holding coordination locks. This inflates scheduling overhead and reduces throughput as worker count grows.

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- Add an in-process queue snapshot cache keyed by file metadata/hash in the orchestration loop, invalidated only when queue/done change.

Expansion:
- Add adaptive polling intervals based on cache staleness and blocker types.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: stale snapshot selecting outdated tasks.
- Mitigation: strict invalidation on mtime/hash changes and lock-bound refresh before committing selection.
evidence: path: crates/ralph/src/commands/run/parallel/worker.rs :: select_next_task_locked :: reloads done and queue with repair+validation every selection attempt.
path: crates/ralph/src/commands/run/parallel/orchestration.rs :: orchestration loop :: repeatedly calls select_next_task_locked while scheduling workers.
workflow: ralph run loop --parallel N with blockers/dependencies :: repeated parse+validate cycles consume scheduler time with little file change between iterations.
plan: Add an internal snapshot cache object for queue/done content plus validation warnings keyed by file fingerprint.
Wire scheduler selection to reuse cached snapshot when fingerprints are unchanged.
Refresh snapshot atomically when fingerprints change before task selection decisions.
Add regression tests for cache hit/miss behavior and stale-invalidation correctness.
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph parallel` and confirming correct task selection with fewer queue reloads under repeated scheduling cycles.

tasks.title: Add shared worktree pool for parallel execution to cut clone overhead
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: performance and cost.

Why it matters:
- Parallel execution currently relies on per-task workspace clone provisioning and reset logic, which increases disk churn and setup latency at higher worker counts.

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- Introduce optional worktree-backed workspace provisioning for parallel runs while keeping existing clone strategy as fallback.

Expansion:
- Add adaptive workspace reuse and cleanup policies based on run volume and branch lifecycle.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: worktree lifecycle edge cases can leak branches/directories.
- Mitigation: explicit cleanup guards, idempotent removal, and integration tests covering interruption/failure cases.
evidence: path: crates/ralph/src/git/workspace.rs :: setup_task_workspace/ensure_workspace_exists :: performs clone/setup/reset workflow per workspace path.
workflow: ralph run loop --parallel 4 :: workspace provisioning cost grows with worker count and task churn.
external: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-worktree :: accessed 2026-02-12 :: git worktree is designed to manage multiple working trees from one repository object store.
plan: Design workspace strategy toggle (clone vs worktree) with safe default and migration path.
Implement worktree create/reuse/remove helpers with robust cleanup and branch naming guarantees.
Integrate strategy selection into parallel orchestration and failure cleanup guards.
Add integration tests for normal completion, interruption, and stale-worktree recovery.
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph parallel` and confirming parallel runs complete with reduced workspace provisioning overhead under worktree mode.

tasks.title: Split oversized `queue.rs` into focused modules to accelerate safe feature work
tasks.description:
Opportunity type: modernization and reliability.

Why it matters:
- `queue.rs` centralizes loading, repair, validation, locking helpers, and large test sections in a 1000+ LOC file, increasing change risk and slowing targeted improvements in queue hot paths.

Proposed approach (minimal slice first):
- Extract cohesive modules (`queue/io`, `queue/repair`, `queue/validation_entry`, `queue/locking_helpers`) while preserving public behavior and existing tests.

Expansion:
- Establish clear ownership boundaries and module-level performance tests for queue read/repair/validate paths.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: refactor churn introduces subtle regressions.
- Mitigation: move code with minimal semantic changes first, then optimize behind existing regression coverage.
evidence: workflow: find crates/ralph/src -name '*.rs' -print0 | xargs -0 wc -l | sort -nr | head :: reports crates/ralph/src/queue.rs at 1064 LOC.
path: crates/ralph/src/queue.rs :: module body :: combines queue I/O, JSON repair logic, validation entry points, and extensive inline tests in one file.
workflow: queue-related enhancements now touch a large mixed-responsibility file, increasing merge conflict and regression risk.
plan: Define target module boundaries and move code mechanically without behavior changes.
Rewire call sites to new module paths while preserving existing public APIs where needed.
Run and expand queue-focused tests after each extraction step to catch regressions early.
Document module responsibilities/invariants at top-of-file for each extracted module.
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph queue::` and `make test`, confirming behavior parity after decomposition.
